Skip to contents
siphon 0.8.0
  • Get started
  • Reference
    • Asynchronous Daemons
    • Extending siphon with Custom Backends
  • Changelog

siphon

siphon is a small runtime for pull-based staged pipelines in R.

It is not a replacement for future, mirai, parallel, or other execution backends. Those tools execute jobs. siphon connects stages, limits active jobs with slots, and lets items move to downstream stages as soon as they are ready.

Installation

Install the development version from GitHub:

# install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_github("rolfsimoes/siphon")

Once published on CRAN, install with:

install.packages("siphon")
library(siphon)

# Define test data
items <- 1:5

# Two-stage pipeline showing flow composition
res <- items |>
  pump(function(x) x + 1, backend = "main") |>
  pump(function(x) x * 2, backend = "main") |>
  pump_run(verbose = FALSE)

# Results in original input order
print(res)
## [[1]]
## [1] 4
## 
## [[2]]
## [1] 6
## 
## [[3]]
## [1] 8
## 
## [[4]]
## [1] 10
## 
## [[5]]
## [1] 12

Features

  • Pull-based staged pipelines - Items move to downstream stages as soon as they are ready, without waiting for upstream completion
  • Multiple execution backends - Support for main thread, mirai, future, and parallel (PSOCK) backends
  • Fault-tolerant execution - parallel_backend() replaces crashed workers and resubmits their jobs (at-least-once semantics)
  • Backpressure control - Bounded buffers with buffer_size to limit memory usage
  • Concurrency control - Per-stage max_workers to manage resource constraints
  • Error handling - Flexible error policies: "collect", "stop", or "continue"
  • Timeout protection - Optional timeout in pump_run() to avoid infinite polling
  • Interactive inspection - pump_step(), pump_peek(), pump_pop(), and a rich print() to step through and debug a live pipeline
  • Status inspection - pump_status() for monitoring pipeline state
  • Order preservation - Results returned in original input order regardless of execution order
  • Custom sources - pump_source() to connect external data sources (queues, databases, files)
  • Streaming drain - pump_drain() for memory-safe daemon-style processing

Use direct parallel map tools when the problem is simply lapply(x, f) with workers. Use siphon when the problem is a staged flow with different resource constraints per stage, such as CPU preparation, main-thread GPU dispatch, and parallel I/O. Note that stages using main_backend() run sequentially on the main thread but do not block pipeline coordination-other stages on async backends continue processing in parallel.

Inspecting a pipeline

A pipeline can be stepped and inspected interactively without consuming it. pump_step() runs beats (advancing work through every stage), print() shows per-stage occupancy, timing, and the bottleneck, pump_peek() looks at ready results without removing them, and pump_run() resumes and finishes the remainder:

p <- 1:6 |>
  pump(function(x) {
    Sys.sleep(0.05)
    x * 2
  }, backend = "main") |>
  pump(function(x) x + 1, backend = "main")

pump_step(p, 3) # advance 3 beats; nothing is consumed
## <pump pipeline>
## ┌─ source (main)   pulled 3/6
## ├─ stage 1 (main)
## │    work [-] 0/1   out [------] 0/6   done 3   err 0
## │    fn 50.1ms/item   coord 0.0ms/beat   beats(3): work 100% starv 0% block 0%
## ├─ stage 2 (main)
## │    work [-] 0/1   out [###---] 3/6   done 3   err 0
## │    fn 0.0ms/item   coord 0.0ms/beat   beats(3): work 100% starv 0% block 0%
## └─ sink   delivered 0/6
print(p) # workers, buffers, per-stage times, beat states
## <pump pipeline>
## ┌─ source (main)   pulled 3/6
## ├─ stage 1 (main)
## │    work [-] 0/1   out [------] 0/6   done 3   err 0
## │    fn 50.1ms/item   coord 0.0ms/beat   beats(3): work 100% starv 0% block 0%
## ├─ stage 2 (main)
## │    work [-] 0/1   out [###---] 3/6   done 3   err 0
## │    fn 0.0ms/item   coord 0.0ms/beat   beats(3): work 100% starv 0% block 0%
## └─ sink   delivered 0/6
pump_peek(p) # look at the next ready result, non-destructively
## [[1]]
## [[1]]$id
## [1] 1
## 
## [[1]]$idx
## [1] 1
## 
## [[1]]$data
## [1] 3
## 
## [[1]]$ok
## [1] TRUE
res <- pump_run(p, verbose = FALSE) # resumes; full ordered result
print(res)
## [[1]]
## [1] 3
## 
## [[2]]
## [1] 5
## 
## [[3]]
## [1] 7
## 
## [[4]]
## [1] 9
## 
## [[5]]
## [1] 11
## 
## [[6]]
## [1] 13

Use pump_pop() only when you intend to consume an item yourself: it removes the item from the stream (a later pump_run() will not return it) and, for managed sources, transfers commit/release responsibility to you.

See the vignette("siphon") for a longer introduction with error handling and status inspection examples.
